definition: The 'fiscal uncanny valley' describes consolidation large enough to contract GDP but insufficient to stabilize the debt trajectory, producing the worst risk-adjusted outcomes. Documented across Eurozone 2010-13, Japan 1997, UK 2010-13, Argentina 2018. Successful consolidations each required at least one of: 200bp+ monetary easing, 10-25% currency depreciation, or external demand boom.
evidence: Four failure cases: Eurozone 2010-13, Japan 1997, UK 2010-13, Argentina 2018 — all showed GDP contraction without term premium compression
Successful counterexamples (Clinton 1993, Canada 1994-97, Sweden 1994-98, Ireland 1987) each required monetary/currency/demand offset
For DOGE 2026: Fed constrained by above-target inflation, dollar reserve status limits depreciation, no external demand boom
Adjusted base rate for pure domestic consolidation without offset: approximately 0/7
Mixing Argentina (EM) with DM cases weakens base rate; Ireland relied on EC structural funds
open_questions: What magnitude of consolidation falls into the uncanny valley vs. being too small to matter?
Can AI capex or other private investment substitute for the missing monetary/currency/demand offsets?
